mission.title: Install and Configure Logging Operator Kube Logging- on Kubernetes
mission.description: The Logging Operator automates the deployment and configuration of a Kubernetes logging pipeline, utilizing Fluent Bit for log collection and forwarding. Installing this operator simplifies the management of logs in your Kubernetes environment, ensuring secure and efficient log handling.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Kube Logging Helm repository to your local Helm configuration to access the Logging Operator chart.
```bash
helm repo add kube-logging https://ghcr.io/kube-logging/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Logging Operator
steps.description:
Install the Logging Operator using Helm, specifying the desired namespace (e.g., 'logging') and version 6.4.0.
```bash
helm install logging-operator kube-logging/logging-operator --namespace logging --create-namespace --version 6.4.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Logging Operator pods are running correctly in the specified namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n logging
```

steps.title: Configure RBAC for the Logging Operator
steps.description:
Ensure that the Logging Operator has the necessary permissions by creating a Role and RoleBinding.
```bash
kubectl create role logging-operator-role --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch,delete --resource=logs --namespace=logging
kubectl create rolebinding logging-operator-rolebinding --role=logging-operator-role --serviceaccount=logging:default --namespace=logging
```

steps.title: Set resource limits
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for the Logging Operator to ensure it runs efficiently. Edit the values.yaml file or use Helm to set the limits during installation.
```bash
helm upgrade logging-operator kube-logging/logging-operator --namespace logging --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of the Logging Operator will show the operator pod running in the 'logging' namespace, and you will be able to access logs from your applications through the configured logging pipeline. Additionally, RBAC and resource limits will be applied as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kube-logging https://ghcr.io/kube-logging/helm-charts
helm repo update

kubectl create role logging-operator-role --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch,delete --resource=logs --namespace=logging
kubectl create rolebinding logging-operator-rolebinding --role=logging-operator-role --serviceaccount=logging:default --namespace=logging
helm upgrade logging-operator kube-logging/logging-operator --namespace logging --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Logging Operator Helm release from the specified namespace to stop all associated resources and deployments.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the 'logging' namespace and verify that all resources have been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and resources of the Logging Operator.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and execute the upgrade command to install the latest version of the Logging Operator.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the Logging Operator pods are running the new version correctly in the specified namespace.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Logging Operator pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors using the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n logging
```

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permission issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter permission errors, ensure that the Role and RoleBinding are correctly set up. Verify with:
```bash
kubectl get role -n logging
kubectl get rolebinding -n logging
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being killed due to Out Of Memory (OOM) issues, consider increasing the memory limits. Check current resource usage with:
```bash
kubectl top pods -n logging
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check for version compatibility and ensure that the Helm repository is updated. Run:
```bash
helm repo update
helm history logging-operator -n logging
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and that Helm and kubectl are installed and configured.
